inlineContent.text: I’m willing to bet that every article you’ve ever read about building a multi-step form in SwiftUI tells you the same thing.
Put the state in some sort of super view model. Share it across screens via
or
or whatever this week’s flavor of cross-view state happens to be.

The super view model owns the data, and the children read and write it through bindings or update it via closures or delegates or in some cases by punting and passing the entire object through to the view’s view model.
And that works.
Sort of.
Until you ship the
second

flow that wants to reuse the same screens, or the third flow whose final step needs to do a network call, or the moment a designer asks for an onboarding sequence that starts in a sheet and continues with pushes. T
hen the parent-owns-everything model starts to wobble.
There’s a better answer. It’s been sitting in
for a while now and I haven’t written about it, and perhaps more to the point I’ve updated it for Version 2.1.
So let’s talk about it.

We’re going to build a four-screen onboarding flow. A welcome screen, a name entry screen, an email screen, and a “you’re all set” confirmation screen. When the user taps Done on the confirmation, the whole thing dismisses back to where it started and hands the collected data to a completion handler the caller passed in.
Sound familiar? It should. Half the apps on your phone open with one.
The naive version puts a class somewhere outside the flow:
You hang that off the parent view, pass it down through
, and write a switch statement somewhere that maps
to a screen. The children mutate the published properties. The parent observes. Eventually the parent unmounts the model and the sequence is over.
Cool. Done, right?
Well, not really.

The class outlives the workflow. The published model exists for the entire time the user is in that part of the app, not just during the onboarding. It’s “shared mutable state” for a thing that should have lasted thirty seconds. And the moment you want a
second
flow that needs three of those same screens, you’re either copying the class or refactoring it into something more general. Neither of which is fun.
So what do we want?

We want the state to live exactly as long as the sequence does. We want it to travel with the navigation, not above it. And we want the type that holds the state to also be the type that decides what comes next, because in practice those two responsibilities are joined at the hip.
That type is
. It’s a protocol. The shape is small.

An associated destination type, a checkpoint property that Navigator manages for you, two methods that drive the sequence forward using our old friend, NavigationDestination, and three optional lifecycle hooks that can be customized as needed.
is
because real world navigation flows often need to await a network call or surface an error before they can decide what comes next.
is generic over the flow type itself. Its terminal cases carry the flow as an associated value, and its
case carries your destination, which by convention will carry the flow forward as well. Whatever work
did to reach a decision travels with the result.
That’s it. The entire protocol.
The interesting bit is what flows that conform to this protocol look like.

code: private let completion: Callback<(String, String, String)>
init(firstName: String = "", completion: @escaping @Sendable (String, String, String) -> Void) {

inlineContent.text: That
at the top isn’t decoration. Because
and
both return
, neither signature mentions
directly, so the compiler can’t infer the associatedtype the way it used to. Declare it.
Look at what isn’t there.
No
, no class, no
, no shared store. The flow is a plain struct. The data the sequence collects lives in stored properties. The rules over that data, like what counts as a valid email, live in computed properties.
The decisions about which screen comes next live in
And the completion callback the caller passed in lives in a private closure that fires from
when the flow ends.
The flow
is
its state.
That’s the entire idea.
The flow type owns the data the sequence collects, the rules that govern that data, the navigation logic that responds to it, and the callback that fires when it’s done.
Four concerns, one type, one storage location, and that storage location lives exactly as long as the sequence itself.
The next step is defining our destination enum. Each case carries the flow and specifies the corresponding view.
So when
returns

, what actually gets pushed onto the navigation path is a snapshot of the flow at that moment. The next view receives that snapshot as a value. It can mutate its copy locally and hand the updated version back to the navigator when the user taps Next.
This is an example of value semantics doing real work. Each pushed step gets its own copy of the flow as it existed at time of its push.
And navigating back works as expected, with the previous views maintaining their own copy of the state.
If you’ve followed along with the rest of this series, none of the
machinery should be surprising. The flow simply defines it.
Here’s what a step view looks like:

code: Section("Please enter your first and last name…") {

inlineContent.text: In this simple case there’s no view model. No shared store. The
binds straight into the flow’s stored properties via
’s projected binding.
When we’re ready to move on, the button wraps
in a
because the navigator’s
is async.
Note that we’re handing our mutated copy back.
Read that again.
That’s the entire data layer for the screen.
We don’t need a
, we don’t need an
, and we don’t need to thread a binding through three levels of view init. The flow walks with the user, the user types into the flow, and when they’re done with that screen they hand the flow back.
Two methods on
move a flow forward.
stamps a checkpoint capturing the current navigator and its path index, then calls
and routes the result.
awaits
and routes the result. Both read the returned
the same way: a
pushes the next step, and a
, or
returns the stack to the checkpoint and fires the matching hook.
The flow’s
is the whole state machine.
Inspect a few fields. Decide what’s next. Return a

. The chain of state checks scales fine for four steps. Past five or six you might want an index, or perhaps an explicit step enum, but until then this style reads about as clearly as state machines get.
The onboarding flow above never mutates anything inside
, so each
case carries the unchanged
. But
is
for a reason. It’s built to
do
work, including async work, and fold whatever it produces back into the flow before handing it off.
The previous example’s
is synchronous because it doesn’t need to await anything. Real flows often do.
is
so it can
a network call, a persistence write, or any other effect that produces information needed to decide which step comes next.
Mutated state forwards through two channels. Destination cases of
carry your destination, and by the convention shown in the
enum, each destination case carries the flow as an associated value. Terminal cases (
) carry the flow directly, so the matching lifecycle hook sees whatever state the work produced.
Let’s look at a more complex example that makes that concrete.

code: let (userID, termsRequired) = try await api.register(email: email, name: firstName)

inlineContent.text: First, notice that the “is this email valid?” question is asked of the flow, not of the view. The flow defines what valid means with
, and
consults it. The view that collects the email doesn’t get to invent its own rule. Validation logic depends on the flow.
Next, if we’re not registered, we make an API call to do so. The result mutates our state.
is a default-implemented helper on
that takes a closure, applies it to a mutable copy of
, and returns the result. It exists specifically so that producing updated state from a non-mutating method stays a one-liner. The returned
is what rides into
or
, and that’s how the next view ends up holding a flow with
set.
If the async work throws, the error propagates out through
to be handled by the view.
If you’d rather treat a failure as fatal to the flow, return
from
instead. The navigator routes that to
after restoring the stack to the checkpoint, and the flow it hands the hook is the one you returned.

Finally, the api call returns whether or not the user should see the terms of agreement view. Perhaps it’s location dependent. But regardless of how that’s determined on the back end, the flow is able to dynamically decide the next view to be shown to the user.
The async work happens before any navigation occurs. The user sees the last screen they were on until
returns. If the work might take a moment, the calling view should reflect that, usually by disabling its action button or showing a progress indicator while its
is in flight.
Now the interesting part.
The flow needs to do something with the data once it’s collected. The naive answer is to pass the handler into the final view as a parameter and let the view call it. Done.
Don’t do that.

The completion screen doesn’t want to know that a callback exists. It wants to know how to dismiss the flow when the user taps Done. That’s it. The callback, the arguments, the data the callback needs, none of that is the view’s problem.
So the closure goes on the flow, private. And the flow fires it from one of the lifecycle hooks the protocol already gives you:

code: private let completion: Callback<(String, String, String)>

inlineContent.text: is exactly what it sounds like. A hook that fires after the flow completes. The view calls
, the navigator restores the stack to the checkpoint, then calls the flow’s
, which fires the closure with the data the flow has been accumulating.
This is the division the protocol was designed around.
and
are decisions about navigation. The hooks are where side effects go. Don’t reach for the view layer to invoke a callback the flow already has all the information to invoke itself.
The view never sees the closure. The closure never appears in a view’s parameter list. The data the closure needs is data the flow already owns. Three concerns kept in three places.
Notice the type.
is a Hashable wrapper around a closure that Navigator ships specifically for these types of situations.
A bare
would have killed the auto-synthesized
conformance on
, because closures aren’t
, and a
flow is what the destination enum needs in order to ride the navigation path.

solves that by hashing on a stable identifier instead of the closure itself, so the flow stays Hashable for free. The closure is fixed for the flow’s lifetime anyway, so identity-based equality is the right semantic.
Callbacks aren’t
. Storing one on the flow disables state restoration for any

that hosts it, and it can interfere with deep linking because external URL handlers can’t synthesize the underlying closure. If state restoration or deep-link reachability matters, use a checkpoint with a typed return value instead.
A flow can end one of two ways. Either
returns
, or
, or a view calls the equivalent imperative method on the navigator directly. Both routes do the same thing: restore the navigation stack to the checkpoint, then call the matching hook on the flow.
The confirmation screen is the natural place for the imperative form. The view knows it’s the last step. The state machine doesn’t need to figure that out.

code: Text("That's all there is to it!")

inlineContent.text: dismisses everything back to the checkpoint and fires
, which is where the caller’s completion handler gets invoked. That’s the whole termination story.
Note that the hook fires
after
the navigator has returned to the checkpoint, not before. If

wants to show a thank-you toast, the user is already back at the entry point, not on the last screen of the flow. That’s almost always what you want, but it’s worth being explicit about so you don’t try to push something onto a stack that no longer exists.
One more thing the simple struct version gets you for free.
The welcome screen opens as a sheet. The rest push inside it. When the flow completes, the navigator dismisses the sheet
and
pops the path back to whatever button started the flow. One mechanism, no coordination between push depth and sheet presentation.
That works because the checkpoint is recorded relative to the navigator that called

. Navigator tracks the full presentation tree, so dismissal traverses it as a single operation rather than as separate “pop the path” and “dismiss the sheet” steps. I covered the dismissal machinery in detail in an earlier article on Navigator’s dismissible tree, so I won’t rehash it here.
is a protocol. Which means your flows themselves can be protocols. Which means you can reuse a single screen across multiple sequences.

Think about an address entry view. The same screen belongs in checkout and in profile editing and in shipping setup and probably two other places. The naive version copies the view three times with three different concrete flow types. The slightly less naive version reaches for a “coordinator” pattern and a pile of type erasure. The clean version is generic over a protocol:
is generic over any flow that satisfies the requirement.
and
both route their address step through the same view. Each one keeps its own
enum and its own
logic. Only the screen itself is shared.
Reuse at the screen layer, specialization at the flow and destination layer. The kind of separation that’s hard to achieve when everything is a concrete class and a published property.
I’ve been needling the coordinator pattern this entire article. Let’s pay that off.

If you’ve been reading along thinking “this is just a coordinator,” you’re half right. The coordinator pattern, popularized in UIKit-land a decade ago and chewed over by every iOS architecture article since, hides navigation from the view. So does a flow. Same goal, same general shape, same
entry point.
The difference is what the type owns.

A coordinator is typically dumb. That’s not an insult; that’s the convention. The coordinator knows how to push screen A, then B, then C. It doesn’t know what data A collected. It doesn’t know whether B’s Next button should be enabled. It doesn’t know how to validate C. Those decisions live in view models or services or wherever you’ve decided to put them. The coordinator is a navigation router and nothing else.

The split sounds clean on a whiteboard. In practice it means two parallel hierarchies, one for navigation and one for state, that have to stay in sync via callbacks, delegate methods, or some flavor of binding. Screen B knows it just collected an address. It tells its view model. Then somebody, usually the view, has to also tell the coordinator “okay, push C now.”
That’s two messages for one event. Which means that it’s twice as many places to forget one.
A flow collapses that. The state goes on the flow. The navigation decision goes on the flow. The view collects data, mutates the flow, and calls one method. The flow’s
reads the state it just received and decides what needs to come next based on that state. One type, one decision, no parallel hierarchy.
That’s why
carries the flow in its terminal cases. That’s why the flow rides through the destination enum as an associated value. That’s why the flow conforms to
All of it serves the same end: the data and the navigation logic are joined at the hip because in real applications they actually are.

What they still have in common is the part that made the coordinator pattern popular in the first place. The email view doesn’t know what comes after submission. It doesn’t know there’s a confirmation screen, or that the welcome step was a sheet, or that completion dismisses everything back to a button two view hierarchies up.
It just calls
and the flow figures it out.
Same information hiding and separation of concerns. Just a smarter type doing the hiding.
So that’s

I covered a fair bit. The flow as state container. The flow as state machine. Validation living with the data it validates. Destinations carrying the flow as an associated value. Views binding straight into the flow. Async work inside
, with mutated state riding back to the next view through the destination’s associated value and to the hooks through
’s terminal cases. The completion handler hidden on the flow and fired from the
lifecycle hook. Explicit completion via
. Reusing views across flows via protocols. Why a flow is a smarter coordinator.

What I didn’t cover: how flows compose, what happens when a flow wants to launch a sub-flow, the lifecycle hook patterns for telemetry and rollback, and the relationship between flow checkpoints and the named checkpoints I covered earlier in this series. Those are their own articles.
So what do you think? Building multi-step sequences with a parent view model and bindings today? Going to try a flow instead? Hit a case where you reached for
and the flow pattern would’ve been cleaner?
Drop me a line. Let me know in the comments below.

metadata.title: Navigation Flows: The Flow Is the State
